old_claim.note: Advanced Materials Science provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim.note: The Comet failures exposed unresolved fatigue and aluminum-alloy behavior in pressurized jet transports; materials-science knowledge accelerated safer jet-airliner scaling rather than making the first service possible.

source_shape.source_locator: Key Safety Issue(s), Precursors, and accident-recommendation sections discussing fatigue, aluminum alloys, stress distribution, and the Comet's role in teaching the industry pressurized-fuselage fatigue behavior.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The FAA account shows that early jet airliner service exposed unresolved materials and fatigue behavior in pressurized fuselages and led to industry learning rather than proving materials science was already a prerequisite.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: The source supports a weaker accelerates edge: materials-science understanding improved safety and scaling after the first service, while the first jetliner existed despite incomplete fatigue knowledge.

ontology_before: Advanced materials science was modeled as a generic enabling dependency for jet airliners, creating a hard-looking chronology relation.
ontology_after: Advanced materials science is modeled as an accelerator for safe scaling of pressurized jet transports after first service.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Graph invariant 2026-06-09-transport-drilling-materials-chronology.json locks this accelerates edge type.
would_reject_if: The node were narrowed to later fail-safe high-cycle jet-airliner designs rather than the first commercial jetliner category.
A stronger aviation source showed the postwar materials-science discipline was necessary before the first Comet service.
The graph split early jetliners from mature safe jet transport systems and moved this edge only to the mature system node.
short_reason: Materials science improved safe jet-airliner scaling but did not make the first jetliner service possible.
